text:
MULTIPARAMETRIC MRI PROSTATE AND PELVIS WITH AND WITHOUT CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION: 71-year-old male with newly diagnosed prostatic adenocarcinoma on TRUS-guided biopsy (Gleason 4+5=9, 10/12 cores positive). PSA 87.4 ng/mL. Referred for local staging and evaluation of regional nodal disease.

TECHNIQUE: Multiparametric MRI of the prostate and pelvis performed on a 3.0 Tesla scanner with endorectal coil and phased-array surface coil. Sequences obtained: axial and coronal T2-weighted imaging of the prostate and pelvis; diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) with apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) mapping (b-values 0, 800, 1400 s/mm2); dynamic contrast-enhanced (DCE) sequences using intravenous gadolinium (Gadavist, 0.1 mmol/kg). No prior prostate MRI available for comparison.

---

FINDINGS:

PROSTATE:
The prostate gland is markedly enlarged, estimated total volume approximately 58 cc. There is a dominant, poorly marginated hypointense mass on T2-weighted imaging centered at the bilateral mid-gland and extending into the right apex, occupying the majority of the peripheral zone bilaterally. The dominant lesion measures approximately 3.4 cm in greatest dimension. The lesion demonstrates markedly restricted diffusion with very low ADC values (estimated ADC nadir in the range of 550 mm2/s) consistent with highly cellular carcinoma. Dynamic contrast enhancement reveals early arterial wash-in with rapid washout, characteristic of aggressive malignancy. Overall PI-RADS assessment category: 5 (clinically significant cancer very likely present). The central gland shows heterogeneous benign prostatic hyperplasia-type changes.

EXTRACAPSULAR EXTENSION (ECE):
There is unequivocal extracapsular extension at the right apical prostate, with irregular contour bulging and obliteration of the rectoprostatic angle on the right. Subtle capsular irregularity and possible early extracapsular spread is also noted at the bilateral mid-gland, most pronounced on the right posterior aspect. The overall extent of ECE is felt to be established rather than equivocal at the right apex.

SEMINAL VESICLES:
Bilateral seminal vesicle involvement is present. There is loss of the normal T2 hyperintensity within both seminal vesicles, replaced by hypointense tumor signal with associated restricted diffusion extending along the seminal vesicle-prostate junction. The involvement is more extensive on the right, where there is near-complete replacement of the right seminal vesicle. The left seminal vesicle shows involvement at its base with partial preservation of normal signal more distally. Findings are diagnostic of bilateral seminal vesicle invasion (T3b).

RECTUM:
The anterior rectal wall abuts the posterior prostate but there is no convincing evidence of direct rectal invasion. The mesorectal fascia appears intact.

BLADDER:
The bladder base and trigone are well-visualized. No direct extension of tumor into the bladder neck is seen. No bladder wall thickening or intraluminal masses.

PELVIC LYMPH NODES:
There is a lymph node in the right obturator fossa measuring 1.8 cm in short axis with a rounded contour, loss of the normal fatty hilum, and mildly heterogeneous internal signal on T2-weighted imaging. This node demonstrates mildly restricted diffusion. Morphologic features are highly suspicious for metastatic nodal involvement. Two additional right external iliac chain lymph nodes are identified, measuring 0.9 cm and 0.7 cm in short axis, respectively. Both demonstrate mild rounding and borderline loss of hilar architecture; the larger of these two (0.9 cm) is indeterminate to mildly suspicious for nodal involvement. A small left external iliac lymph node measuring 0.5 cm is present and appears morphologically benign. No left-sided obturator or internal iliac adenopathy.

BONY PELVIS:
The osseous structures of the pelvis are imaged on these sequences. No focal marrow signal abnormality suspicious for osseous metastatic disease is identified within the pelvis, sacrum, or imaged proximal femora. No cortical destruction.

OTHER PELVIC STRUCTURES:
No pelvic free fluid. No pelvic sidewall invasion. The neurovascular bundles are closely abutted bilaterally and are likely involved given the extent of posterolateral tumor; nerve-sparing surgery would not be appropriate given disease extent.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Bulky bilateral prostatic adenocarcinoma with a dominant lesion measuring approximately 3.4 cm, PI-RADS 5, occupying the peripheral zone bilaterally with dominant involvement of the right apex and bilateral mid-gland.

2. Definite extracapsular extension at the right apex; additional sites of capsular irregularity and possible ECE at the bilateral mid-gland posteriorly.

3. Bilateral seminal vesicle invasion, right greater than left, consistent with T3b disease.

4. Right obturator lymph node (1.8 cm) with morphologic features strongly suspicious for metastatic involvement. Two additional right-sided iliac chain lymph nodes (0.9 cm, 0.7 cm) are indeterminate; the 0.9 cm node is borderline suspicious.

5. No pelvic osseous lesions identified on MRI sequences.

6. Overall staging impression: at minimum cT3bN1, high-risk locally advanced prostate cancer. Correlation with systemic staging imaging (bone scan and cross-sectional CT of chest/abdomen/pelvis) is recommended to exclude distant metastatic disease.

Reporting Radiologist: [Signature on file]
Division of Abdominal Imaging / Genitourinary Radiology
